schools.summary: Matthew Henry: Dinah was, for aught that appears, Jacob's only daughter, and we may suppose her the mother's fondling and the darling of the family, yet she proves neither safe nor a comfort by her venturing abroad; Gill notes she was the daughter of Leah, born to Jacob; the witnesses note Dinah, Leah's daughter, going out to see the daughters of the land — the unguarded going-forth that opened the door to disaster (the dark chapter opening with a seemingly innocent act: 'Dinah the daughter of Leah, which she bare unto Jacob, went out to see the daughters of the land'; the imprudence the commentators mark, that Dinah, a young girl, perhaps fifteen or sixteen, and her father's only daughter, 'went out' alone beyond the safety of the family tents to 'see the daughters of the land,' the women of Shechem, out of curiosity to view their dress, customs, and ways; the danger in such wandering, that a daughter of the covenant family, mingling unguarded with the daughters of a heathen city, exposed herself to peril; the lesson, as Henry notes, that those who needlessly venture into the company and amusements of the world's daughters often find neither safety nor comfort there, so Dinah's unguarded going-out is the fatal first step of the tragedy).

themes: Dinah the daughter of Leah, which she bare unto Jacob
went out to see the daughters of the land
Dinah's unguarded going-out into a heathen city
the imprudent first step that opened the door to disaster

schools.summary: Gill: Shechem was the son of Hamor, from whom the city had its name, near which Jacob and his family now were; he was prince of the country; Keil and Delitzsch note this occurred in the interim between Dinah's coming and the later events; the witnesses note Shechem, the son of Hamor the Hivite, prince of the country, seeing Dinah, taking her, lying with her, and defiling her — the grievous outrage, a prince abusing his power to violate the maiden (the going-out met by a great wrong: 'Shechem the son of Hamor the Hivite, prince of the country, saw her, he took her, and lay with her, and defiled her'; the gravity of the crime, that Shechem, 'prince of the country' and so a man of rank and power, 'saw her' and, his lust kindled, 'took her,' seized her by force or persuasion, 'lay with her, and defiled her,' robbing her of her chastity; that his rank made the wrong the worse, abusing the power that should have protected to violate a stranger maiden; that the Scripture plainly names it a defiling, a real sin and outrage, however the chapter goes on to tell of Shechem's after-love; the danger Dinah's wandering had brought upon her, so the prince of Shechem violates Dinah, the great wrong of the chapter).

themes: Shechem the son of Hamor the Hivite, prince of the country, saw her
he took her, and lay with her, and defiled her
the prince abusing his power to violate Dinah
a real and grievous sin, the great wrong of the chapter

schools.summary: Gill: his inclination was to her, she was always in his thoughts; it was not a mere lustful desire that was suddenly raised and soon over, but a constant and continued affection; the witnesses note Shechem's soul cleaving to Dinah, loving the damsel and speaking kindly to her — the strange after-love of the offender, a real attachment following the outrage (the wrong done, Shechem's heart bound to Dinah: 'his soul clave unto Dinah the daughter of Jacob, and he loved the damsel, and spake kindly unto the damsel'; the unusual turn, that Shechem's was not the common case of lust that, once satisfied, turns to loathing as Amnon's toward Tamar, but a real and lasting attachment, 'his soul clave unto Dinah,' she was ever in his thoughts, 'he loved the damsel' with a true affection, and 'spake kindly unto' her, seeking to win her heart and comfort her; that this after-love, though it could not undo the wrong, showed Shechem desired to make Dinah his wife and not to cast her off; the mingling of real love with the guilt of the deed, so Shechem, having wronged Dinah, now loves her and seeks her in marriage).

themes: his soul clave unto Dinah the daughter of Jacob
and he loved the damsel, and spake kindly unto the damsel
Shechem's real and lasting after-love for Dinah
not the common lust that turns to loathing, yet the wrong remained

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch: Shechem spake unto his father Hamor, saying, Get me this damsel to wife; Gill: Shechem told his father the whole affair, at least what a strong affection he had for Dinah; the witnesses note Shechem asking his father Hamor to get him Dinah for a wife — the offender seeking to wed the maiden he had wronged through his father's suit (Shechem, his heart fixed, turning to his father to make the match: 'Shechem spake unto his father Hamor, saying, Get me this damsel to wife'; the manner of it, that Shechem, in the custom of the time, does not himself approach Dinah's family but asks his father Hamor, the prince, to negotiate and obtain her for him in marriage; that this shows the depth and seriousness of Shechem's attachment, that he would have Dinah not as a passing conquest but as a lawful 'wife,' and engages his father's authority to secure the match; that, while this desire to marry was in itself honourable, it could not erase the wrong already done nor make the union welcome to Jacob's house, so Shechem sends his father to seek Dinah for his wife).

themes: And Shechem spake unto his father Hamor
Get me this damsel to wife
Shechem seeking Dinah in marriage through his father
the depth of attachment shown, though the wrong remained

schools.summary: Gill: the report of this was brought to Jacob, very probably by one of the maids which attended Dinah to the city, for it was hardly to be thought she could carry the tidings herself; his sons being in the field with the cattle, Jacob held his peace until they came; the witnesses note Jacob hearing that Dinah was defiled, his sons being in the field, and holding his peace until they returned — the grieved father's restraint, waiting in silence for his sons before acting (the news coming to Jacob, who keeps his counsel: 'Jacob heard that he had defiled Dinah his daughter: now his sons were with his cattle in the field: and Jacob held his peace until they were come'; the father's grief and self-command, that Jacob 'heard' of the great wrong done his daughter, yet, his 'sons' being away 'with his cattle in the field,' he 'held his peace until they were come,' restraining his sorrow and saying nothing rashly; the prudence in this silence, that Jacob would not act alone or in haste but waited for his sons to take counsel together how to deal with so grave a matter; that this restraint, wise in itself, gave room for his sons' rasher passions to take the lead when they returned, so Jacob, grieved, holds his peace and waits for his sons).

themes: Jacob heard that he had defiled Dinah his daughter
and Jacob held his peace until they were come
the grieved father's restraint, waiting for his sons
prudence in silence, taking counsel rather than acting in haste

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch: Hamor the father of Shechem went out unto Jacob to commune with him; Gill: Hamor went out unto the tent of Jacob, without the city; the witnesses note Hamor, Shechem's father, going out to Jacob to commune with him — the prince coming in person to seek the alliance, the negotiation begun (Hamor, at his son's request, coming to open the matter: 'Hamor the father of Shechem went out unto Jacob to commune with him'; the courtesy and gravity of the visit, that Hamor, the prince, does not summon Jacob but himself 'went out unto Jacob,' to his tent outside the city, to 'commune with him,' to treat with him about the marriage of Shechem and Dinah; that the father came to plead his son's suit and to seek the alliance, an honourable form however the matter stood; that this communing was the beginning of the negotiation in which Jacob's sons would soon take their fatal part, so Hamor comes to Jacob to treat for the marriage).

themes: Hamor the father of Shechem went out unto Jacob
to commune with him
the prince coming in person to seek the alliance
the negotiation begun in which the sons would take their fatal part

schools.summary: Gill: as soon as the sons of Jacob heard of the abuse of their sister they came from the field; the men were grieved and very wroth at so great an indignity done to their sister and family; the Scripture owns the wrong, that Shechem had wrought folly in Israel; the witnesses note the sons of Jacob coming from the field, grieved and very wroth, because folly had been wrought in Israel which ought not to be done — the just indignation of the brothers at the foul wrong done their sister (the sons returning to a household stunned by the wrong: 'the sons of Jacob came out of the field when they heard it: and the men were grieved, and they were very wroth, because he had wrought folly in Israel in lying with Jacob's daughter; which thing ought not to be done'; the rightness of their first feeling, that the brothers were 'grieved,' deeply pained at the dishonour of their sister and family, and 'very wroth,' justly angered at so foul a crime, and the Scripture itself owns the justice of their grief, that Shechem 'had wrought folly in Israel,' a grave and shameful sin against the covenant family, 'which thing ought not to be done'; that this indignation, in itself just and proper, was the seed of a revenge that would far overshoot the bounds of justice; that just anger, as Calvin notes, ought to have been governed and not let loose to cruelty, so the brothers' just grief and wrath are kindled at the outrage).

themes: the men were grieved, and they were very wroth
because he had wrought folly in Israel... which thing ought not to be done
the just indignation of the brothers at the outrage
right in itself, yet the seed of a revenge that would overshoot justice

schools.summary: Calvin: though the sons of Jacob were justly incensed, yet their indignation ought to have been appeased, or at least somewhat mitigated, by Hamor's words; Gill: Hamor communed with Jacob and his sons, who came in just at that time; the witnesses note Hamor telling them that his son Shechem longed for Dinah and asking that she be given him to wife — the father's earnest suit, pressing his son's love to win the marriage (Hamor, the brothers now present, making his plea: 'The soul of my son Shechem longeth for your daughter: I pray you give her him to wife'; the manner of the suit, that Hamor pleads his son's deep love, 'the soul of my son Shechem longeth for your daughter,' and earnestly begs, 'I pray you give her him to wife,' seeking by fair and humble words to win the family's consent and turn the wrong into a marriage; that Hamor's words, as Calvin notes, owning the love and seeking an honourable union, were such as ought to have softened the brothers' anger or at least mitigated it and disposed them to a calmer answer; that the suit, however well-meant on Hamor's part, was met not with honesty but with the brothers' hidden treachery, so Hamor earnestly sues for Dinah, pressing his son's love).

themes: The soul of my son Shechem longeth for your daughter
I pray you give her him to wife
Hamor's earnest suit, pressing his son's love
words that should have softened the brothers, met with treachery

schools.summary: Gill: there was no objection on Hamor's side, it lay on the other, for Abraham was charged by him not to take a wife of the Canaanites to his son Isaac, and the same charge was given Jacob by Isaac; the witnesses note Hamor proposing that the two families intermarry, giving and taking daughters — the offer of full alliance by intermarriage, which struck against the covenant family's separation (Hamor, to seal the union, proposing a general intermarriage: 'make ye marriages with us, and give your daughters unto us, and take our daughters unto you'; the breadth of the offer, not merely Dinah for Shechem but a full and mutual intermarrying of the two peoples, Jacob's house giving their daughters to the Shechemites and taking the Shechemites' daughters in return, that the families might be wholly joined; the deep difficulty this raised for Jacob's house, that such intermarriage with the Canaanites was the very thing the patriarchs had forbidden, Abraham to Isaac and Isaac to Jacob, lest the holy seed be mingled with the heathen and corrupted; that the offer, fair in worldly terms, struck against the separation God required of the covenant family, so Hamor proposes a full intermarriage that the covenant family could not rightly accept).

themes: make ye marriages with us
give your daughters unto us, and take our daughters unto you
Hamor's offer of full intermarriage between the peoples
striking against the separation the patriarchs required

schools.summary: Charles Ellicott: Hamor proposes that Jacob's family abandon their nomad life and settle among the Shechemites as fellow-citizens; Gill: they should dwell with them peaceably and quietly, not as sojourners only but as inhabitants; the witnesses note Hamor offering Jacob's family to dwell, trade, and get possessions in the land — the generous offer of full settlement and citizenship, tempting them to abandon the pilgrim life (Hamor sweetening his suit with worldly advantage: 'ye shall dwell with us: and the land shall be before you; dwell and trade ye therein, and get you possessions therein'; the liberality of the offer, that Hamor invites Jacob's family to 'dwell with us' not as mere strangers but as settled fellow-citizens, the whole 'land... before you' to range freely, to 'dwell,' to 'trade,' carry on commerce and prosper, and to 'get... possessions,' acquire lands and property as their own; that this meant, as Ellicott notes, abandoning the pilgrim nomad life of the patriarchs for a settled place among the Shechemites; the snare hidden in the kindness, that such settlement and mingling with the heathen would have drawn the covenant family from their separated calling and their hope of the promised inheritance, so Hamor offers a tempting home that would have cost them their pilgrim calling).

themes: ye shall dwell with us: and the land shall be before you
dwell and trade ye therein, and get you possessions therein
Hamor's generous offer of settlement, trade, and possessions
a snare hidden in the kindness, costing them their pilgrim calling

schools.summary: Gill: Shechem said this to the father and brethren of Dinah; he addressed them after his father Hamor had done speaking; the witnesses note Shechem asking to find grace in their eyes and promising to give whatever they should say — the offender's humble, lavish offer to win the family's consent (Shechem himself adding his own earnest plea to his father's: 'Let me find grace in your eyes, and what ye shall say unto me I will give'; the eagerness of his suit, that Shechem, longing for Dinah, begs to 'find grace' in the eyes of her father and brothers and offers a blank promise, 'what ye shall say unto me I will give,' to meet any terms, pay any price, hold nothing back, if only he may have her; the seriousness of his love shown in this lavish readiness, that he would give whatever they named; the tragic irony, that this open and generous offer, made in earnest, was met by the brothers with a hidden and deadly deceit, naming a condition not to win the marriage but to work a slaughter, so Shechem humbly and lavishly offers to give whatever they ask).

themes: Let me find grace in your eyes
and what ye shall say unto me I will give
Shechem's eager, lavish offer for Dinah
an open suit met, in tragic irony, by hidden deceit

schools.summary: Gill: the dowry was what a man gave to a woman at her marriage, for in those times and countries the man gave the dowry, not the woman; Shechem bids them fix it at as high a rate as may be thought fit, 'multiply them exceedingly'; the witnesses note Shechem offering whatever dowry and gift they should name, only that they give him Dinah to wife — the lavish bid for Dinah, Shechem holding nothing too dear to win her (Shechem pressing his offer to the utmost: 'Ask me never so much dowry and gift, and I will give according as ye shall say unto me: but give me the damsel to wife'; the custom and the lavishness, that in those times the man gave the 'dowry,' the bride-price, to the woman's family, and Shechem bids them set it as high as they will, 'ask me never so much,' promising to 'give according as ye shall say,' to pay any sum and add any 'gift' however great; the single thing he asks in return, 'but give me the damsel to wife,' all his lavish offers aiming at this one end, to have Dinah; that Shechem's readiness to pay any price shows the strength of his desire, yet, as the brothers' answer will show, no price could undo the wrong or make the match acceptable to the deceiving sons, so Shechem offers any price for Dinah).

themes: Ask me never so much dowry and gift
but give me the damsel to wife
Shechem's lavish bid, offering any price for Dinah
the strength of his desire, yet no price could undo the wrong

schools.summary: Gill: they answered deceitfully, proposing the marriage of their sister on terms after mentioned, when they never intended it should ever be; Onkelos, Jonathan, and Jarchi interpret it that they spoke with guile or wisdom; the witnesses note the sons of Jacob answering Shechem and Hamor deceitfully, because Dinah had been defiled — the turning-point of treachery, the brothers cloaking a deadly plot in fair words (the Scripture plainly naming the sin of the answer that follows: 'the sons of Jacob answered Shechem and Hamor his father deceitfully, and said, because he had defiled Dinah their sister'; the frank condemnation in the text itself, that the brothers' answer was given 'deceitfully,' with guile, proposing terms, the circumcision of the men, which they never meant as a true condition of marriage but only as a snare to disable the Shechemites for slaughter; the motive named, 'because he had defiled Dinah their sister,' the real wrong that fired their hearts, yet which did not justify the treachery of the means; that here the chapter turns from the wrong done to Dinah to the greater evil now plotted by her brothers, a deceit that abused even the sign of the covenant, so the brothers answer with a deceit that masked a deadly design).

debates.question: How does Scripture frame the brothers' answer?

sides.position: Plainly as deceit — they answered 'deceitfully,' proposing terms they never meant to keep, a snare to disable the Shechemites; the real grievance was no warrant for the treachery

themes: the sons of Jacob answered Shechem and Hamor his father deceitfully
because he had defiled Dinah their sister
the turning-point of treachery, a plot cloaked in fair words
the grievance real, yet no warrant for the deceit

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch: they said they could not give their sister to one uncircumcised, for that were a reproach; Gill: this was the answer of Levi and Simeon to Hamor and Shechem; the witnesses note the brothers saying they could not give their sister to an uncircumcised man, for that would be a reproach — the deceitful plea that wore the dress of religious scruple (the brothers cloaking their guile in a show of piety: 'We cannot do this thing, to give our sister to one that is uncircumcised; for that were a reproach unto us'; the cunning of the plea, that there was a real truth in it, that for the covenant family to give a daughter to the uncircumcised heathen was indeed unfitting and 'a reproach,' but the brothers used this true scruple deceitfully, not from religious care but as the bait of their snare, to require the circumcision of the men with no honest intent of marriage; the great evil in this, that they abused circumcision, the holy sign of God's covenant, making the sacred token a mere instrument of revenge; that to cover a murderous design with the language of religious scruple was to add hypocrisy and profanation to treachery, so the brothers plead religious scruple to mask their deadly snare).

themes: We cannot do this thing, to give our sister to one that is uncircumcised
for that were a reproach unto us
the deceitful plea dressed in religious scruple
abusing circumcision, the covenant sign, as the bait of a snare

schools.summary: Jamieson, Fausset and Brown note the significance of names and signs among this people, the brothers seizing on the sign of circumcision as their pretended condition; Gill: the condition was that Dinah should be given in marriage upon their being circumcised; the witnesses note the brothers consenting only on condition that every male be circumcised — the snare set, circumcision demanded not for faith but to disable the men (the brothers naming the fatal condition: 'in this will we consent unto you: If ye will be as we be, that every male of you be circumcised'; the craft of the demand, that the brothers offer to 'consent' to the marriage and alliance on one condition, 'that every male of you be circumcised,' to 'be as we be,' outwardly conformed to the covenant people; the hidden design, that circumcision was demanded not, as it seemed, that the Shechemites might truly join the covenant and worship the true God, but as a snare, the men, sore and weakened by the operation, being unable to defend themselves and so lying open to the brothers' swords; the deep wickedness of using God's holy ordinance as a trap, making the sign of life the instrument of death, so the brothers set the snare under the guise of a condition of marriage).

themes: in this will we consent unto you
If ye will be as we be, that every male of you be circumcised
circumcision demanded as a snare, not for faith
God's holy sign made an instrument of death

schools.summary: Gill: by 'our daughters' they meant Dinah, whom they call their daughter, because she was the daughter of their family and because they were treating in the name of their father and in conformity to his desire; the witnesses note the brothers promising, on condition of circumcision, to give and take daughters, dwell together, and become one people — the fair promise of full union held out as the bait, never meant to be kept (the brothers painting the reward of the bargain: 'Then will we give our daughters unto you, and we will take your daughters to us, and we will dwell with you, and we will become one people'; the apparent generosity of the promise, that once the men were circumcised the brothers would 'give our daughters,' Dinah and others, and 'take your daughters,' 'dwell with you,' and 'become one people,' everything Hamor had asked, a full and friendly merging of the two peoples; the hollowness of it all, that these fair words of union were the very bait of the snare, the promise of all that Shechem and Hamor desired held out to draw them and their city into the trap with no intention of ever fulfilling it; that the brothers spoke in their father's name and the family's, binding all in a promise they meant to break, so the brothers hold out a fair union as the lure of their deceit).

themes: Then will we give our daughters unto you, and we will take your daughters to us
and we will dwell with you, and we will become one people
the fair promise of full union held out as bait
never meant to be kept, the lure of the deceit

schools.summary: Adam Clarke: it is natural to suppose that the tribe of Hamor was numerous, and Jacob's family comparatively small, so the threat to depart was a way to press the condition; Gill: the alternative was that, if they would not agree to circumcision, the brothers would take Dinah and be gone; the witnesses note the brothers saying that, if the men would not be circumcised, they would take Dinah and depart — the firm alternative that pressed the bargain, the brothers feigning readiness to leave (the brothers closing their proposal with a threat of withdrawal: 'if ye will not hearken unto us, to be circumcised; then will we take our daughter, and we will be gone'; the pressure in the alternative, that the brothers make the circumcision the absolute condition, declaring that, if it were refused, they would simply 'take our daughter,' Dinah then in Shechem's house, 'and we will be gone,' breaking off all dealing and departing; that this firm stand was meant to make the bargain seem the more genuine and to press Hamor and Shechem to accept, lest they lose Dinah and the alliance both; the cunning that wove even this show of readiness to leave into the snare, making the trap appear an honest ultimatum, so the brothers press the condition with a threat to take Dinah and go).

themes: if ye will not hearken unto us, to be circumcised
then will we take our daughter, and we will be gone
the firm alternative that pressed the bargain
making the snare appear an honest ultimatum

schools.summary: Gill: the condition proposed was acceptable to both, and they agreed to comply with it, Hamor because of the great love he had for his son, Shechem because of the love he had for Dinah; the witnesses note the brothers' words pleasing both Hamor and Shechem, who agreed to the condition — the snare gladly received, the offered terms welcomed by the unsuspecting father and son (the deceitful proposal gladly embraced: 'their words pleased Hamor, and Shechem Hamor's son'; the readiness of both to accept, that the terms, which seemed to open the way to all they desired, the marriage, the alliance, the merging of the peoples, 'pleased Hamor' and his son Shechem, who saw in them no snare but a fair bargain; the motives the commentators mark, that Hamor was moved by 'his great love for his son,' willing to do anything to win Dinah for him, and Shechem by his own love for the damsel; the tragic blindness in this, that the father and son, suspecting no guile, welcomed the very condition that would be their ruin, the bait gladly swallowed, so Hamor and Shechem are pleased and ready to comply with the snare).

themes: their words pleased Hamor
and Shechem Hamor's son
the deceitful terms gladly received by the unsuspecting
the bait swallowed, who saw a fair bargain, not a snare

schools.summary: Gill: the young man deferred not to be circumcised himself and to get all the males of the city circumcised; he delayed not a moment but made all the haste he could to get it accomplished; the witnesses note Shechem not deferring to do the thing, for he delighted in Dinah, being more honourable than all his father's house — the eager, honourable youth hastening to his own undoing for love of Dinah (Shechem, his heart set on Dinah, losing no time: 'the young man deferred not to do the thing, because he had delight in Jacob's daughter: and he was more honourable than all the house of his father'; the eagerness of his love, that Shechem 'deferred not' but at once set about being circumcised himself and bringing the whole city to it, so great was his 'delight in Jacob's daughter' that he would spare no haste to win her; the testimony to his standing, 'he was more honourable than all the house of his father,' a man of weight and esteem in the city whose word carried, which is noted to explain how readily the citizens followed him; the tragic irony, that this honourable young man's very eagerness and influence hastened the whole city's destruction, his love speeding him and his people to ruin, so Shechem, honourable and eager, hastens to fulfil the fatal condition).

themes: the young man deferred not to do the thing, because he had delight in Jacob's daughter
and he was more honourable than all the house of his father
Shechem's eager haste, sparing no time for love of Dinah
his honour and influence, in tragic irony, hastening the city's ruin

schools.summary: Gill: the gate was where courts of judicature were held and all public affairs respecting the common interest of the city were transacted; there Hamor, their prince, and Shechem laid the matter before the citizens; the witnesses note Hamor and Shechem coming to the gate of their city to commune with the men — the prince and his son bringing the proposal before the assembled citizens to win their consent (Hamor and Shechem taking the matter to the place of public counsel: 'Hamor and Shechem his son came unto the gate of their city, and communed with the men of their city'; the place and the purpose, that 'the gate of their city' was the seat of justice and public business where the citizens gathered and weighty affairs were decided, and there Hamor, the prince, and Shechem 'communed with the men of their city,' laying before the assembled townsmen the proposed alliance with Jacob's family and its condition; that, since every male in the city must be circumcised, the consent of all the men was needed, and so the prince and his honoured son came to persuade them; that the leaders themselves, all unknowing, were leading their whole city into the trap, so Hamor and Shechem bring the proposal to the city gate to win the citizens).

themes: Hamor and Shechem his son came unto the gate of their city
and communed with the men of their city
the prince and his son persuading the citizens at the gate
unknowingly leading their whole city into the trap

schools.summary: Gill: by 'these men' they meant Jacob and his sons, pointing to their tents which were near their city, and no doubt more was said than is here expressed; the witnesses note Hamor and Shechem telling the citizens that Jacob's men were peaceable, the land large enough, and urging mutual marriages — the persuasive speech that commended the strangers and the union to the city (the prince and his son pressing the proposal upon the townsmen with fair reasons: 'These men are peaceable with us; therefore let them dwell in the land, and trade therein... let us take their daughters to us for wives, and let us give them our daughters'; the arguments urged, first that 'these men are peaceable,' Jacob's family being no threat but quiet, well-disposed neighbours, so they might safely 'dwell in the land, and trade therein,' especially as 'the land... is large enough for them,' room for all without crowding, and second the gain of intermarriage, 'let us take their daughters... and let us give them our daughters,' a full and profitable joining of the two peoples; the irony, that the very thing urged as safe and profitable, that the 'peaceable' strangers should dwell and intermarry, would prove the city's destruction, so Hamor and Shechem commend the alliance as safe and gainful to the city).

themes: These men are peaceable with us
let us take their daughters to us for wives, and let us give them our daughters
Hamor and Shechem commending the alliance as safe and gainful
the very thing urged, in irony, would prove the city's ruin

schools.summary: Gill: the only term or condition insisted upon, to come into alliance and affinity with them, was that every male be circumcised; the witnesses note Hamor and Shechem telling the citizens that the one condition of the union was that every male be circumcised — the single condition set before the city, the snare passed on by the unknowing leaders (the prince and his son naming the one price of the alliance: 'Only herein will the men consent unto us for to dwell with us, to be one people, if every male among us be circumcised, as they are circumcised'; how lightly the condition is presented, 'only herein,' as if it were a small and single thing, the sole term on which Jacob's family would 'consent... to dwell with us, to be one people'; that Hamor and Shechem, themselves deceived, pass the deceit on to their city, urging the men to undergo circumcision 'as they are circumcised,' that the union and all its supposed profit might be had; the tragic blindness of all, leaders and people alike taking for a small price what would be their slaughter, the snare presented as a trifling condition, so the one condition is set before the city as the price of the union).

themes: Only herein will the men consent unto us for to dwell with us, to be one people
if every male among us be circumcised, as they are circumcised
the single condition passed to the city by the unknowing leaders
the deadly trap urged as a trifling price

schools.summary: Gill: they suggested that Jacob's cattle and substance and every beast would in course come into their families in process of time, by intermarrying with them, or by their being more numerous and powerful than they; the witnesses note Hamor and Shechem urging that Jacob's cattle and substance would become theirs if they consented and the strangers dwelt with them — the appeal to covetous gain, holding out Jacob's wealth as the citizens' reward (the leaders clinching their argument with a promise of profit: 'Shall not their cattle and their substance and every beast of theirs be ours? only let us consent unto them, and they will dwell with us'; the covetous bait now offered the city, that Hamor and Shechem suggest that, by this alliance and intermarriage, Jacob's 'cattle,' 'substance,' and 'every beast' would in time 'be ours,' flowing into the Shechemites' families and hands whether by marriage or by their greater numbers; the appeal to greed, that the men were drawn to 'consent' not chiefly for the marriage but for the gain, the hope of enriching themselves with the wealthy strangers' goods; the irony again, that those who hoped to swallow up Jacob's substance would themselves be swallowed up in slaughter, so the citizens are lured by the hope of gaining Jacob's wealth).

themes: Shall not their cattle and their substance and every beast of theirs be ours?
only let us consent unto them, and they will dwell with us
the appeal to covetous gain, holding out Jacob's wealth
the men lured by greed, not chiefly the marriage

schools.summary: Gill: all the inhabitants of the city who came to the gate of it, upon the summons given them, hearkened and were circumcised, and departed from thence; the witnesses note all that went out of the gate of the city hearkening to Hamor and Shechem, and every male being circumcised — the whole city won over and submitting, walking unknowing into the trap (the persuasion succeeding and the city complying in full: 'unto Hamor and unto Shechem his son hearkened all that went out of the gate of his city; and every male was circumcised'; the completeness of the compliance, that 'all that went out of the gate,' all the men of the city who had gathered and now departed to do it, 'hearkened' to their prince and his son, won by the arguments of safety and gain, and 'every male was circumcised'; the influence of the leaders and the lure of profit, which together carried the whole city to submit to the painful rite; the dreadful position they were now in, every man sore and helpless, the whole male population disabled at once, the snare fully closed, all unknowing, so the entire city is circumcised and lies open to the coming blow).

themes: hearkened all that went out of the gate of his city
and every male was circumcised
the whole city submitting to the rite
the male population disabled at once, the snare fully closed

schools.summary: Gill: it was on the third day, when their pains were strong upon them, as the Targum of Onkelos, or when they were weak through the pain of circumcision, as the Targum of Jonathan; the witnesses note Simeon and Levi, on the third day when the men were sore, taking each his sword, coming upon the city boldly, and slaying all the males — the treacherous massacre, the snare sprung when the city lay helpless (the trap now sprung in blood: 'on the third day, when they were sore, that two of the sons of Jacob, Simeon and Levi, Dinah's brethren, took each man his sword, and came upon the city boldly, and slew all the males'; the cold cruelty of the deed, that the brothers waited for 'the third day, when they were sore,' the wound at its worst, the men disabled and unable to resist, and then 'Simeon and Levi, Dinah's brethren,' took their swords, 'came upon the city boldly,' without fear the men being helpless, and 'slew all the males'; the aggravations of the sin, that it was treachery, the men slain under a covenant of peace, cruelty, a whole city for one man's crime, and sacrilege, the holy sign of circumcision made the very means of the slaughter; that Jacob himself, on his deathbed, would curse this cruel anger (Genesis 49:5-7), so Simeon and Levi work a treacherous and bloody massacre on the helpless city).

debates.question: How do the witnesses judge Simeon and Levi's massacre?

sides.position: A treacherous, cruel, and sacrilegious deed — slaying a whole city under a covenant of peace, for one man's crime, abusing the sign of circumcision; Jacob himself cursed this anger (Genesis 49:5-7)

themes: Simeon and Levi, Dinah's brethren, took each man his sword
and came upon the city boldly, and slew all the males
the treacherous massacre of the helpless, circumcised city
a deed of treachery, cruelty, and sacrilege Jacob would curse

schools.summary: Gill: they slew Hamor and Shechem his son, with whom they had been just treating in a seeming friendly manner; Shechem was the chief aggressor and his crime was very heinous, but considering the manner of the revenge it is to be weighed; the witnesses note Simeon and Levi slaying Hamor and Shechem with the sword and taking Dinah out of Shechem's house — the killing of the prince and his son and the rescue of their sister from the offender's house (the brothers singling out the chief men: 'they slew Hamor and Shechem his son with the edge of the sword, and took Dinah out of Shechem's house, and went out'; the two acts, the slaying of 'Hamor and Shechem,' the prince and the very man who had wronged Dinah, with whom the brothers had just been treating in a show of friendship, and the recovery of 'Dinah out of Shechem's house' where she had been kept, leading her away; the mixture in the deed, that Shechem, the chief aggressor, was indeed guilty and his crime heinous, so that his death was not without a cause, yet the treacherous manner, and the slaughter of the whole innocent city with him, turned a just cause into a great wickedness; that the rescue of Dinah was right but the bloody way of it was not, so the brothers slay the prince and his son and bring Dinah away).

themes: they slew Hamor and Shechem his son with the edge of the sword
and took Dinah out of Shechem's house, and went out
the killing of the chief men and the rescue of Dinah
a just cause turned to wickedness by the treacherous manner

schools.summary: Gill: the rest of the sons, as the Targum of Jonathan paraphrases it, understanding what their two brothers had done, came and joined them and partook of stripping the slain and spoiling the city; the witnesses note the sons of Jacob coming upon the slain and spoiling the city because their sister had been defiled — the rest of the brothers joining to plunder, the revenge widening into pillage (the other sons now sharing in the deed: 'the sons of Jacob came upon the slain, and spoiled the city, because they had defiled their sister'; the widening of the crime, that what Simeon and Levi began with the sword, 'the sons of Jacob,' the rest of the brothers, now completed with plunder, coming 'upon the slain' and 'spoiling the city' of all its goods; the reason again given, 'because they had defiled their sister,' that the brothers held the one wrong done to Dinah as warrant for the whole bloody and rapacious revenge; that the defiling of Dinah was a real and grievous wrong but could not justify the massacre of a city nor the plundering of the innocent; how the brothers' just grief had now swollen into wholesale cruelty and greed, so the sons of Jacob spoil the slaughtered city).

themes: The sons of Jacob came upon the slain, and spoiled the city
because they had defiled their sister
the rest of the brothers joining to plunder
the revenge widening from the sword into pillage

schools.summary: Gill: the Shechemites had hoped to have the cattle and substance of Jacob's family, and in a hypocritical manner submitted to circumcision for the sake of worldly advantage, and now they lost their own; the witnesses note the brothers taking the sheep, oxen, asses, and all that was in the city and field — the seizing of all the flocks and goods, the spoilers stripped of what they had coveted (the plunder total: 'they took their sheep, and their oxen, and their asses, and that which was in the city, and that which was in the field'; the completeness of the spoil, that the brothers carried off 'their sheep,' 'oxen,' and 'asses,' and 'that which was in the city,' the goods of the houses, 'and that which was in the field,' the standing cattle and produce, sweeping away the whole substance of the Shechemites; the striking turn of providence the commentators mark, that the Shechemites had submitted to circumcision in part out of greed, hoping that 'their cattle and substance... be ours,' to swallow up Jacob's wealth, and now by a just retribution they lost their own, stripped of all they had coveted from others; that covetousness brought its own punishment, so the brothers seize the whole substance of the city).

themes: They took their sheep, and their oxen, and their asses
and that which was in the city, and that which was in the field
the total plunder of the city's substance
the Shechemites who coveted Jacob's wealth stripped of their own

schools.summary: Gill: 'all their wealth,' or power or strength, everything that made them mighty and powerful, their gold and silver, jewels, and rich furniture of their houses, their arms and weapons of war, their goods; the witnesses note the brothers taking captive all the wealth, the little ones, and the wives, and spoiling all that was in the houses — the carrying away of the helpless survivors and the last of the goods, the cruelty at its height (the spoil carried to its bitter end: 'all their wealth, and all their little ones, and their wives took they captive, and spoiled even all that was in the house'; the totality and the cruelty, that beyond the cattle the brothers seized 'all their wealth,' the gold, silver, jewels, and rich goods, and took 'all their little ones, and their wives... captive,' carrying off the helpless widows and orphans of the men they had slain, and 'spoiled even all that was in the house,' leaving nothing; the height of the wrong, that not content with the slaughter of the men they made captives of the innocent women and children and stripped the houses bare; how far the revenge had overrun all bounds of justice, falling on the guiltless, so the brothers carry off the wealth, women, and children, the spoil complete).

themes: all their wealth, and all their little ones, and their wives took they captive
and spoiled even all that was in the house
the carrying off of the helpless survivors and the goods
the revenge overrunning all bounds, falling on the guiltless

schools.summary: Adam Clarke: Jacob says they have troubled him, brought his mind into great distress and endangered his personal safety, and made him to stink, to be abhorred, among the people of the land; the witnesses note Jacob rebuking Simeon and Levi for troubling him and making him odious among the Canaanites, who might gather and destroy him and his house — the father's grieved rebuke, weighing the deadly peril their deed had brought (Jacob, returning to find the bloody work done, sharply reproving the two: 'Ye have troubled me to make me to stink among the inhabitants of the land... and I being few in number, they shall gather themselves together against me, and slay me; and I shall be destroyed, I and my house'; the substance of the rebuke, that Jacob charges them that they have 'troubled' him, brought him into great distress and danger, and made him 'to stink,' odious and abhorred, 'among the Canaanites and the Perizzites,' the peoples of the land, and he fears the consequence, that, 'being few in number,' he and his house would be no match for the surrounding nations who might now 'gather... together against' them to take vengeance and 'slay' and 'destroy' them all; that some have observed Jacob's rebuke dwells on the danger and dishonour more than on the sin and cruelty themselves; that his fear was real, yet God would protect him, the next chapter showing a terror of God falling on the cities, so Jacob rebukes his sons for the deadly peril their cruelty had brought).

debates.question: What does Jacob's rebuke chiefly weigh?

sides.position: The danger and dishonour — that the deed made him odious among the nations and endangered his few household; some note he dwells on the peril more than on the sin and cruelty themselves
On his deathbed (Genesis 49:5-7) Jacob does condemn the cruelty and anger itself, cursing it

themes: Ye have troubled me to make me to stink among the inhabitants of the land
they shall gather themselves together against me... and I shall be destroyed, I and my house
Jacob's grieved rebuke of the deadly peril
dwelling on the danger and dishonour their deed had brought

schools.summary: Gill: they answered in a very pert and unseemly manner, should he deal with their sister as with a harlot, make a whore of her and then keep her in his house as such, is this to be borne with?; the witnesses note Simeon and Levi answering that Shechem should not have dealt with their sister as a harlot — the brothers' bold self-justification, pleading the wrong as warrant for the whole revenge (the chapter closing with the brothers' unrepentant reply: 'Should he deal with our sister as with an harlot?'; the spirit of the answer, that Simeon and Levi, far from owning any fault, justify themselves with a defiant question, pleading the foulness of the wrong done Dinah, should Shechem be suffered to treat 'our sister as with an harlot,' to violate her and then keep her as if she were a common woman, and the family bear it tamely; the real grievance beneath the defiance, that the wrong was indeed grievous and the sense of their sister's dishonour was natural, yet that this just feeling was no answer to Jacob's charge, for it could not justify the treachery, the massacre of a whole city, and the plunder of the innocent; that the brothers had the better of the grievance but not of the cause, a real wrong avenged in a wicked way, so the brothers close with a defiant plea that excused not their cruelty).

themes: And they said, Should he deal with our sister as with an harlot?
the brothers' defiant self-justification
a real grievance pleaded as warrant for the revenge
yet no answer to Jacob's charge, no justifying of the treachery
